patterns.rootCause: Permission denied when accessing {path}. The current user or process lacks the required read/write/execute permissions.
patterns.prescription: Check file ownership and permissions with `ls -la {path}`. Fix with `chmod` or `chown`, or run with elevated privileges if appropriate. Avoid running as root unless necessary.

conditions.rootCause: Permission denied in node_modules at {path}. This often happens when node_modules was installed with a different user or with sudo.
conditions.prescription: Delete node_modules and reinstall without sudo: `rm -rf node_modules && npm install`.

conditions.rootCause: Permission denied in the npm cache directory {path}. The cache was likely created by root or another user.

patterns.rootCause: Port {port} is already in use by another process. A server or service is already listening on this port.
patterns.prescription: Find the process using port {port} with `lsof -i :{port}` (macOS/Linux) or `netstat -ano | findstr :{port}` (Windows), then stop it or use a different port.

conditions.rootCause: Port 3000 is already in use. This is the default port for many dev servers (React, Next.js, Express).
conditions.prescription: Kill the existing process on port 3000 or set a different port via the PORT environment variable: `PORT=3001 npm start`.

patterns.match: (?:FATAL ERROR.*?|JavaScript )?(?:heap out of memory|ENOMEM|Cannot allocate memory|allocation failed|out of memory)
patterns.rootCause: The process ran out of available memory (heap). This can happen with large datasets, memory leaks, or insufficient system memory.
patterns.prescription: Increase the Node.js heap size with `--max-old-space-size`, or investigate memory leaks using `--inspect` and Chrome DevTools Memory profiler.
patterns.codeBlock:
# Increase Node.js memory limit to 4GB
node --max-old-space-size=4096 your-script.js

# For npm scripts
NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=4096 npm run build

conditions.when: heap out of memory contains build
conditions.rootCause: Build process ran out of memory. Large projects with many dependencies or complex bundling can exceed default memory limits.
conditions.prescription: Increase memory for the build: `NODE_OPTIONS=--max-old-space-size=8192 npm run build`. Consider code-splitting or lazy imports to reduce bundle size.

patterns.rootCause: Connection refused to {host}:{port}. The target service is not running, not listening on the expected port, or is blocked by a firewall.
patterns.prescription: Verify the service at {host}:{port} is running and accepting connections. Check if the host/port configuration is correct and that no firewall is blocking the connection.

conditions.rootCause: Connection refused to PostgreSQL at {host}:5432. The database server is not running or not accepting connections.
conditions.prescription: Start the PostgreSQL service: `sudo systemctl start postgresql` or `brew services start postgresql`. Verify pg_hba.conf allows connections from your host.
conditions.codeBlock:
# Linux
sudo systemctl start postgresql
sudo systemctl status postgresql

# macOS
brew services start postgresql

conditions.rootCause: Connection refused to Redis at {host}:6379. The Redis server is not running.
conditions.prescription: Start the Redis service: `sudo systemctl start redis` or `brew services start redis`.
conditions.codeBlock:
sudo systemctl start redis
redis-cli ping

conditions.rootCause: Connection refused to MongoDB at {host}:27017. The MongoDB server is not running.
conditions.prescription: Start MongoDB: `sudo systemctl start mongod` or `brew services start mongodb-community`.
conditions.codeBlock:
sudo systemctl start mongod
mongosh --eval 'db.runCommand({ ping: 1 })'

patterns.rootCause: Operation timed out{target}. The remote server or resource did not respond within the allowed time.
patterns.prescription: Check network connectivity and DNS resolution. Increase timeout values if the operation legitimately takes longer. Verify the target service is healthy.

conditions.rootCause: npm registry request timed out. The npm registry may be slow or your network may have connectivity issues.
conditions.prescription: Try setting a longer timeout: `npm config set fetch-timeout 120000`. Or use a different registry: `npm config set registry https://registry.npmmirror.com`.
conditions.codeBlock:
npm config set fetch-timeout 120000
npm cache clean --force
npm install

patterns.match: (?:ENOSPC|No space left on device|disk full|not enough (?:disk )?space)
patterns.rootCause: No disk space remaining. The filesystem is full and cannot write new data.
patterns.prescription: Free disk space by removing unnecessary files. Check disk usage with `df -h` and find large files/directories with `du -sh * | sort -rh | head -20`.
patterns.codeBlock:
# Check disk usage
df -h

# Find large directories
du -sh /* 2>/dev/null | sort -rh | head -20

# Common cleanup
npm cache clean --force
docker system prune -a
rm -rf /tmp/*

conditions.rootCause: System ran out of inotify watchers, not actual disk space. Too many files are being watched for changes.
conditions.prescription: Increase the inotify watch limit: `echo fs.inotify.max_user_watches=524288 | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf && sudo sysctl -p`.
conditions.codeBlock:
echo fs.inotify.max_user_watches=524288 | sudo tee -a /etc/sysctl.conf
sudo sysctl -p

patterns.rootCause: Cross-Origin Resource Sharing (CORS) error. The browser blocked a request from {origin} because the server did not include the appropriate Access-Control-Allow-Origin header.
patterns.prescription: Configure the server to include CORS headers for {origin}. Add `Access-Control-Allow-Origin` and related headers to the server response.

conditions.rootCause: CORS error from localhost during development. The API server does not allow requests from the local dev server.
conditions.prescription: Add localhost to the server's CORS allowed origins, or use a proxy in your dev server configuration (e.g., `proxy` in package.json for CRA, `rewrites` in next.config.js for Next.js).

patterns.rootCause: SSL/TLS error when connecting to {host}. The certificate may be expired, self-signed, or the TLS handshake failed.
patterns.prescription: Check the SSL certificate validity for {host}. If using a self-signed certificate in development, configure your client to trust it. Do NOT disable SSL verification in production.
patterns.codeBlock:
# Check certificate
openssl s_client -connect {host}:443 -servername {host}

# For Node.js with self-signed certs (development only!)
NODE_TLS_REJECT_UNAUTHORIZED=0 node app.js

conditions.rootCause: The SSL certificate for {host} has expired.
conditions.prescription: Renew the SSL certificate. If using Let's Encrypt: `sudo certbot renew`. If using a commercial CA, request a new certificate.
conditions.codeBlock:
sudo certbot renew
sudo systemctl reload nginx

conditions.rootCause: The server at {host} is using a self-signed certificate that is not trusted by the client.
conditions.prescription: In development, add the self-signed CA to your trust store. In production, use a certificate from a trusted CA like Let's Encrypt.
conditions.codeBlock:
# Add self-signed cert to Node.js
NODE_EXTRA_CA_CERTS=./my-ca.crt node app.js

patterns.match: (?:SyntaxError: )?(?:Unexpected token|JSON\.parse|JSON Parse error|Unexpected end of JSON).*?(?:position (\d+))?.*?(?:in (.+))?
patterns.rootCause: JSON parsing failed at position {position} in {file}. The input is not valid JSON — possibly truncated, contains comments, or has a trailing comma.
patterns.prescription: Validate the JSON input. Check for trailing commas, comments (not allowed in JSON), single quotes (must use double quotes), or truncated data. Use a JSON linter to find the exact issue.

conditions.rootCause: package.json contains invalid JSON. This is often caused by trailing commas, merge conflicts, or manual editing errors.
conditions.prescription: Open package.json and fix the syntax error. Look for trailing commas, missing commas between entries, or unresolved merge conflict markers (<<<<<<).

patterns.rootCause: Character encoding error in {file}. The file or data stream contains bytes that are not valid in the expected encoding (usually UTF-8).
patterns.prescription: Check the file encoding with `file -bi {file}`. Convert to UTF-8 if needed using `iconv`. Ensure your application reads files with the correct encoding specified.

patterns.match: (?:EFBIG|file too large|payload too large|entity too large|request entity too large|ERR_FS_FILE_TOO_LARGE).*?(?:(\d+)\s*(?:bytes|MB|GB|KB))?
patterns.rootCause: File or request payload is too large ({size}). The file exceeds the maximum allowed size for the operation.
patterns.prescription: Reduce the file size, increase the server's upload limit, or implement chunked uploads for large files. For Express: set `limit` in body-parser. For Nginx: set `client_max_body_size`.

patterns.rootCause: Process was killed by signal {signal}. This typically means the OS or container orchestrator terminated the process due to excessive memory usage (OOM) or an explicit kill command.
patterns.prescription: If killed by OOM (signal 9 / exit code 137), increase memory limits or fix memory leaks. If killed by SIGTERM (signal 15), check if a process manager or orchestrator is shutting it down.
patterns.codeBlock:
# Check if OOM killer was involved (Linux)
dmesg | grep -i 'oom\|killed process'

# Increase Docker memory limit
docker run --memory=4g your-image

# Increase Node.js heap
node --max-old-space-size=4096 app.js

patterns.rootCause: Version mismatch for {package}: expected {expected} but got {actual}. The installed version is incompatible with what is required.
patterns.prescription: Install the correct version: `npm install {package}@{expected}`. Check your package.json engines field and ensure your runtime matches.
patterns.codeBlock:
# Install specific version
npm install {package}@{expected}

# Check current versions
node -v
npm -v
npx {package} --version

conditions.rootCause: Node.js version mismatch. The project requires Node.js {expected} but the current version is {actual}.
conditions.prescription: Use nvm to switch Node.js versions: `nvm install {expected} && nvm use {expected}`. Update the engines field in package.json if the constraint is wrong.

patterns.match: (?:peer dep(?:endency)?|conflicting|ERESOLVE|Could not resolve|unable to resolve dependency tree).*?(?:['"]?([\w@/.\-]+)['"]?)
patterns.rootCause: Dependency conflict involving {package}. Multiple packages require different incompatible versions of the same dependency.
patterns.prescription: Try `npm install --legacy-peer-deps` to bypass peer dependency checks, or resolve the conflict by aligning versions. Use `npm ls {package}` to see the dependency tree.
patterns.codeBlock:
# See the conflict
npm ls {package}

# Install with legacy peer deps
npm install --legacy-peer-deps

# Or force resolution
npm install --force

conditions.rootCause: React peer dependency conflict. A library requires a different version of React than what is installed.
conditions.prescription: Check which version of React the conflicting package supports and either upgrade/downgrade React or find a compatible library version.
conditions.codeBlock:
npm ls react
npm ls react-dom

patterns.rootCause: Network unreachable when trying to connect to {host}. There is no network route to the destination, or the network interface is down.
patterns.prescription: Check your network connectivity. Verify DNS resolution with `nslookup {host}`. Check if a VPN or proxy is required. Test with `ping {host}` and `traceroute {host}`.

conditions.rootCause: Cannot reach the npm registry. Your network may be down, a proxy/firewall may be blocking the connection, or npm's registry may be experiencing issues.
conditions.prescription: Check npm registry status at https://status.npmjs.org/. If behind a proxy, configure npm: `npm config set proxy http://proxy:port`.
conditions.codeBlock:
npm config set proxy http://your-proxy:port
npm config set https-proxy http://your-proxy:port
